coredata.dc:title: Diamond-like carbon structure-doped carbon dots: A new class of self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescence materials toward light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huiyu
Zhang, Zhaoxuan
Ding, Jie
Xu, Yang
Chen, Guangrui
Liu, Jiale
Zhao, Li
Huang, Ning
He, Zhongyu
Li, Yi
Ding, Lan
coredata.dc:description: The construction of simple and low-cost light-emitting diode (LED) based on carbon dots (CDs) powder is an important and challenging task. Here, diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure-doped carbon dots (D-CDs) powder was prepared within 8 min via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis route with a high production yield of 67.8%. The obtained D-CDs powder emits bright solid-state fluorescence without any other additional solid matrices and exhibits a quantum yield of 67.7%. Self-quenching effect caused by direct π-π interactions and fluorescence resonance energy transfer is strongly suppressed by the sp3 and sp2 carbon hybrid structure and the single energy level in the D-CDs powder. The D-CDs powder was directly employed as phosphors in LED, and bright blue LED was successfully constructed with maximum luminance achieving 3922 cd m−2. More importantly, the introduction of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure opens a new era for the preparation of CDs powder with solid-state fluorescence.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of LED based on D-CDs
Solid state fluorescence mechanism of D-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra for the D-CDs solution. Inset is a photograph of the D-CDs solution. (B) Excitation and emission spectra for the D-CDs powder. Inset is a photograph of the D-CDs powder. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of the D-CDs solution and D-CDs powder. (D) Excitation-independent emission spectra of the D-CDs solution. (E) TEM image of the D-CDs. Inset: size distribution histogram (above) and HRTEM image of the D-CDs (below). (F) SAED pattern of the D-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XRD (A), Raman (B) and 13C NMR (C) spectra of the D-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectra of CDsR1, CDsR2, CDsR3 and D-CDs. (E) The QY of CDsR1, CDsR2, CDsR3 and D-CDs under different N, B and sp3C content. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS survey scan and high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s and B1s of CDsR1 (A), CDsR2 (B), CDsR3 (C) and D-CDs (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Emission spectrum of the LED prepared by different mass ratio of D-CDs powder. (B) The CIE chromaticity diagram for the illustrating LED. Inset: the image of the corresponding LED. (C) Emission spectrum of the LED sample 3 (mass ratio: 15%) operating at different voltage. (D) Spectral composition of different colors for the LED based on the D-CDs powder. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Emission spectra comparison of the LED after 4 months of daylight storage. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fluorescence decay lifetimes and fit parameters of the D-CDs solution and D-CDs powder.
Summarized synthesis conditions of CDsR1, CDsR2, CDsR3 and D-CDs.
Photoelectric parameters of LED with different mass ratio of the D-CDs.
Photoelectric parameters of LED sample 3 operating at different voltages.
Comparison of the photoelectric parameters of LED based on the D-CDs powder with different LED in literature.

head.ce:title: Diamond-like carbon structure-doped carbon dots: A new class of self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescence materials toward light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Sciences, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics, College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory on Integrated Optoelectronics
College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The construction of simple and low-cost light-emitting diode (LED) based on carbon dots (CDs) powder is an important and challenging task. Here, diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure-doped carbon dots (D-CDs) powder was prepared within 8 min via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis route with a high production yield of 67.8%. The obtained D-CDs powder emits bright solid-state fluorescence without any other additional solid matrices and exhibits a quantum yield of 67.7%. Self-quenching effect caused by direct π-π interactions and fluorescence resonance energy transfer is strongly suppressed by the sp3 and sp2 carbon hybrid structure and the single energy level in the D-CDs powder. The D-CDs powder was directly employed as phosphors in LED, and bright blue LED was successfully constructed with maximum luminance achieving 3922 cd m−2. More importantly, the introduction of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure opens a new era for the preparation of CDs powder with solid-state fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting diode (LED) is the ideal light of the 21st century, and have attracted tremendous attention due to their superior luminous efficiency, long lifetime, and low power consumption [1,2]. Generally, the most popular phosphors for commercial LED mainly rely on rare-earth materials [3]. However, rare-earth materials are suffered from high cost and scarce in resources [4]. In recent years, semiconductor quantum dots are considered to be the potential substitute phosphors for LED [5], but the complicated preparation procedures and the involvement of toxic heavy metal ions have limited their large-scale applications [6]. Therefore, the search for environmentally friendly and low cost fluorescent materials suitable for light-converting phosphors for LED is an significant and challenging task.

Carbon dots (CDs) proved to be a promising candidate for LED due to their low toxicity, facile preparation, and attractive photoluminescence properties [7]. However, CDs always undergo aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) in solid-state, which is ascribed to the excessive direct π-π interactions and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), limiting their application in LED [8]. The typical approach to constructing CDs-based LED is to disperse CDs solution in solid matrices, such as starch [9], agar [10], inorganic salts [11], mesoporous aluminas [6], nanofiber [12], silica xerogel [13], and certain polymers [14]. In this case, the fluorescence of CDs is preserved by uniform dispersion and space limitation, because the matrices play the role of media like water. These methods typically involve complicated preparation processes and high production costs [10]. Undoubtedly, the best way is to synthesize pure CDs with solid-state fluorescence and directly apply the CDs powder as phosphors for LED [15]. Although few efforts have been made to achieve solid-state fluorescence of CDs, the development is still in its early stage [8,16–20]. Many problems remain to be solved; especially the low quantum yield (QY), low production yields and color variation of CDs in solid state [21]. It is still highly desirable to develop a simple and economic strategy for large-scale preparation of CDs powder with high QY and investigate the related mechanism.

In this work, a simple and cost-effective method to inhibit the ACQ effect of CDs in solid state was proposed. Diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure-doped carbon dots (D-CDs) powder was directly synthesized via a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis route in a reducing solvent (formaldehyde) using potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP), sodium azide (NaN3) and boric acid (BA) as precursors. Compared with other techniques (such as hydrothermal [22], ultrasonic [23] and solid-state pyrolysis methods [24]), the proposed method exhibit many advantages including faster and simpler synthesis procedure, larger synthesis scale as well as much higher production yield (67.8%). Moreover, the synthesized D-CDs not only have excitation-independent emission and a pure single lifetime without any further purification, but also exhibit ultra-high QY in both solid state and aqueous solution. After further characterization, the structure and the solid-state fluorescence mechanism of the D-CDs were discussed. Finally, D-CDs powder was satisfactorily applied to the construction of blue LED as light-converting phosphors without sacrificing their fluorescence characteristics. In this report, a new class of D-CDs with solid-state fluorescence was synthesized, which opened a new gate to effectively avoid ACQ effect of CDs.

KHP was purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). NaN3 was obtained from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). BA was provided by Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Formaldehyde was acquired from Liaoning Quanrui Reagent Co., Ltd (Jinzhou, China). All chemicals are analytical grade without any purification. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA). A stock solution of D-CDs with a concentration of 20.2 mg mL−1 was prepared by dissolving the D-CDs powder in ultra-pure water.

The morphology and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the D-CDs was carried out by a JEM-2100 transmission election microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectrum of the D-CDs was acquired from a XRD-6100 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Raman spectrum of the D-CDs was recorded by a LabRAM AMAMIS smart spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 633 nm (HJY, France). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured by an Avance spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using D2O as the solvent. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a Nicolet 360 spectrometer (Madison, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB250 electron spectrometer (Thermo Electron, USA). Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by an F-2700 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence lifetimes and absolute quantum yields (QYs) were measured by a FLS 920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). Thermogravimetry (TG) analysis was performed by a TGA Q500 Thermal Analyzer Instrument (TA, USA). The emission colors and spectra of the LED device were identified using the Commission International d’Eclairage (CIE) colorimetry system.

For the preparation of the D-CDs powder, KHP (0.5 g), NaN3 (0.5 g) and BA (1.0 g) were dissolved in 5.0 mL formaldehyde. The mixture was subject to ultrasound for 5 min to form a transparent solution and then transferred into a glass beaker. After heating at 750 W in a domestic microwave oven for 8 min, D-CDs powder with high QY was directly obtained. The production yield of the D-CDs powder was calculated to be 67.8 ± 3.4%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of LED based on D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: GaN LED chip with an emission peak of 365 nm and operating voltage of 4.0 V was purchased from Advanced Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd for providing excitation light. The D-CDs powder was mixed with AB epoxy silicone resin (mass ratio of the D-CDs powder = 15%). The mixture was coated onto the GaN LED chip and thermally cured in a 60 °C oven for 1 h.

D-CDs powder exhibits good solubility in water. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the D-CDs solution (Fig. 1 A) exhibits two peaks at 232 and 270 nm, which are assigned to the π-π* transition of C=C and the aromatic rings [25]. The excitation and emission spectra of the D-CDs solution is illustrated in Fig. 1A with an excitation peak centered at 326 nm and an emission peak centered at 413 nm. D-CDs solution shows a bright blue emission under UV light (inset in Fig. 1A) and a QY of 73%. It is worth noting that the D-CDs powder exhibits bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) illumination and its QY (67.7%) is largely retained. Furthermore, there is not any significant shift in the excitation and emission spectra of the D-CDs powder compared with that of D-CDs solution (Fig. 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid state fluorescence mechanism of D-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the fluorescence of CDs is largely quenched when they are dried into powder [26]. One of the most encouraging results of this study is that the D-CDs powder still exerts strong fluorescence under UV light (inset in Fig. 1B), and the QY of the D-CDs powder (67.7%) is nearly the same as the D-CDs solution. In general, ACQ effect of CDs powder is due to the existence of additional energy transfer, including FRET and direct π-π interaction, resulting in the red shift of the fluorescence spectrum, significant decreases of QY and fluorescence lifetimes [27]. As shown in Fig. 1A, the overlap between the emission and the excitation spectra of the D-CDs is very small, which largely inhibits the FRET process. Fluorescence lifetimes and fluorescence dynamics of the D-CDs solution and D-CDs powder were also investigated to understand the high QY of the D-CDs powder. The fluorescence decay curves of the D-CDs solution and D-CDs powder are presented in Fig. 1C and the radiative rate (k
r ) and non-radiative rate (k
nr ) of the D-CDs solution and the D-CDs powder were calculated from Eqs. (1) and (2) according to fluorescence lifetime (τ) and QY:
(1)
k
r
k
r
+
k
n r
= Q Y
(2)
k
r
+
k
n r
=
1
tτ

As shown in Table 1 , both the D-CDs solution and the D-CDs powder exhibit single exponential lifetimes (τ) of 6.97 ns and 7.15 ns, respectively. Unique excitation-independent emissions (Fig. 1D) and the single exponential lifetime of the D-CDs demonstrate the sole energy level of the D-CDs [16], which inhibits the FRET process of the D-CDs and lead to high QY. Furthermore, the k r and k nr of D-CDs powder do not change significantly compared with that of the D-CDs solution. The subtle differences in τ and k
nr between the D-CDs powder and the D-CDs solution verify the suppression of FRET process in D-CDs powder since FRET will lead to decrease of τ and increase of k nr of CDs [3,28].

The morphology of the D-CDs was characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1E, the D-CDs present nearly spherical shape and dispersed uniformly without apparent aggregation. The particle size distribution of the D-CDs (upper inset in Fig. 1E) was obtained by counting the size of 200 particles, indicating the diameter of the D-CDs is mainly distributed in 1.7–3.5 nm with an average size of 2.43 ± 0.39 nm. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals the polycrystalline phase hybrid structure of the D-CDs (lower inset in Fig. 1E). The D-CDs exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes of 0.20 nm and 0.24 nm, which may be attributed both to the (102) diffraction plane of graphite and to the (103) lattice of nanodiamond [29]. Since their lattice fringes are very close to each other, it is sometimes difficult to distinguish them on the basis of lattice fringes. However, the SAED pattern excludes the presence of nanodiamond, since the SAED pattern of the D-CDs only shows an inconspicuous diffused halo (Fig. 1F), which is not typical for nanodiamonds [30].

In order to figure out the incomplete crystallization, the structure of the D-CDs was further explored using various spectroscopic and spectrometric techniques including XRD, Raman and NMR spectroscopy. XRD pattern (Fig. 2 A) of the D-CDs displays two distinct diffraction peaks centered at 27.2° and 43.9°, demonstrating the existence of amorphous sp2 and sp3 carbon [31,32]. The Raman spectrum of the D-CDs (Fig. 2B) displays two typical characteristic peaks at 1421 and 1598 cm−1, which are assigned respectively to D-band (sp3C) and G-band (sp2C) stretching [33,34]. The properties between those of nanodiamond and those of graphite of the D-CDs reveal that the D-CDs powder consists of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure, which is in accordance with the literature [35,36] and results previously reported using XPS [37] and Raman spectroscopy [38].

NMR is an effective approach to confirm the existence of sp3C and sp2C. The 13C NMR signals in the range of 0–80 ppm, 100–140 ppm and 170–190 ppm are corresponded to sp3C, sp2C and C=O carboxylic/carbonyl carbon, respectively [39,40]. Fig. 2C depicts the 13C NMR spectrum of the D-CDs. The presence of a signal at 177 ppm can be originated from carbonyl carbon. Internal C=C (sp2C) produces signals are presented in 120–140 ppm. Additionally, the signal at 80 ppm arises from sp3C was also detected. They were established during the carbonization process because there are no sp3C in the production precursors for the D-CDs. The results are consistent with the TEM, XRD, and Raman observations, indicating the diamond-like carbon (sp3C)-doped structure of the D-CDs [41].

To further understand the origin of the high QY of the D-CDs powder, CDsR1, CDsR2 and CDsR3 powders were prepared with different precursors under the same conditions for comparison (Table 2 ). The fluorescence peaks position of CDsR1, CDsR2 and CDsR3 powders are similar to that of the D-CDs powder (Fig. S1). However, the QYs of CDsR1, CDsR2 and CDsR3 powders are 8.3%, 15.4% and 13.1%, respectively, which are far less than that of the D-CDs powder (Fig. 2E). The QYs of the CDsR2 and CDsR3 powders are improved compared with that of the CDsR1 powder, demonstrating that the introduction of either NaN3 or BA can increase the QY of the CDs powder. Thus, when NaN3 and BA were introduced simultaneously, the D-CDs powder with extremely high QY was obtained. These results suggest that the simultaneous introduction of NaN3 and BA has benefit for the increase of the QY of the D-CDs powder.

The chemical compositions and functional groups of the CDsR1, CDsR2 and CDsR3 and D-CDs powders were explored via FT-IR (Fig. 2D) and XPS (Fig. 3 ). Broad absorption band at 3345 cm−1 is assigned to the vibrational stretching of OH/−HN [42]. The vibration of N3 originated from NaN3 is located at 2114 cm−1 [43]. The sharp peaks at 1765-1490 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration of C=C, C=N and C=O [44,45]. Peaks at 1475-1185 cm−1 can be originated from CN, BN, CH bonds [46–48]. In addition, peaks between 1163 and 757 cm−1 are attributed to the vibration of BO, CB, and CO [47]. By comparing the FT-IR spectra of these four CDs, the FT-IR spectrum of the CDsR2 powder shows the absorption band corresponding to N3 (2114 cm−1) [43], which indicates the remains of NaN3. It can be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of the D-CDs powder, when NaN3 and BA were introduced simultaneously, the absorption peak of N3 is vanished and the absorption peak of Β−N is aroused (1350 cm−1) [49]. These results suggest that BA can react with NaN3 and contribute to the decomposition of NaN3, which improves the QY of the D-CDs.

As can be observed in Fig. 3D, the XPS survey spectrum of the D-CDs demonstrates the presence of C, O, N and B elements. The high resolution spectrum of the C1s indicates the presence of CB (284.1 eV) [49], sp2C (284.6 eV) [50], CO/sp2CN (285.4 eV) [51,52], sp3CN (288.2 eV) [52] and C=O (288.6 eV) [53]. Moreover, the peak at 285.0 eV corresponding to sp3C indicates the successful incorporation of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure in the D-CDs powder [54], which is in agreement with the above results excellently. The high resolution spectrum of the O1s shows three peaks at 531.3, 532.4 and 535.8 eV, which are correspond to C=O, CO and O=CN, respectively [55,56]. The high resolution N1s spectrum of the D-CDs contains only one peak at 399.8 eV, corresponding to NC [57]. The high resolution B1s spectrum of the D-CDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks for BC (191.7 eV), BN (192.2 eV) and BO (192.8 eV) [58,59]. Compared with the XPS measurements of simple mixed product of KHP, NaN3 and BA (Fig. S2), the generation of the chemical bonds (CB, sp3C, sp2CN, sp3CN, O=CN, NC, BC and BN) in the D-CDs illustrates the occurrence of carbonization process between KHP, NaN3 and BA and further demonstrates the successful doping of N and B elements.

By comparing the high resolution spectrum of the C1s, we have found that the peak corresponding to diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure (285.0 eV) is detectable in all these four CDs. However, the content of sp3C is obviously different in these four CDs. Compared with the CDsR1 powder, which exhibit a sp3C content of 8.4% and a QY of 8.3% (Fig. 3A), the CDsR2 powder (Fig. 3B) synthesized with the introduction of NaN3 exhibiting higher sp3C content (28.7%) and QY (15.4%). Similarly, with the participating of BA, sp3C content (10.1%) and QY (13.1%) of the CDsR3 powder are also improved compared with the CDsR1 powder (Fig. 3C). And when NaN3 and BA are jointed simultaneously, as-prepared D-CDs powder show the highest sp3C content (32.5%) and QY (67.7%) among the four CDs powders (Fig. 3D). Obviously, the incorporation of NaN3 and BA contributes to the increase of the sp3C content of the D-CDs powder, thereby achieving an increase in QY. To continue to explore the contributions of NaN3 and BA, N 1s spectra of the CDsR2 and D-CDs powders were compared. Double-peak at 400.3 and 404.5 eV is assigned to N3 [43] and the peak at 399.8 eV is derived from NC [60]. The signal of N3 can be identified in the spectrum of the CDsR2 powder, which indicates the incomplete decomposition of NaN3. With the introduction of BA, the signal of N3 vanishes while the peak represents BN bond (192.2 eV) arises [59] in the XPS spectra of the D-CDs powder. These results are consistent with the FT-IR results thus further confirm that the participation of BA contributes to the decomposition of NaN3, which improves the content of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure and significantly increases the QY of the D-CDs powder.

Based upon these results, we believe that the carbonization of KHP with a rigid planar structure under microwave-assisted conditions forms a stable carbon skeleton. Formaldehyde provides a reducing environment, which enables the reducing of the sp2C in KHP, making the conversion from sp2C to sp3C possible. As the reaction progresses, the decomposition of NaN3 liberates a large amount of heat, accompanied with the production of nitrogen, which offer energy for the reaction while also providing an oxygen-free circumstance to avoid excessive oxidation of the carbon skeleton. BA serves as a synergistic agent, promoting the decomposition of NaN3 and further raising the content of sp3C. The π-conjugation (sp2C) in the D-CDs powder is always cut by the non-conjugated diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure, and the regions of π-conjugation are kept separate from each other. The charge transfer between the regions of π-conjugation is blocked due to the insulator properties of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure [36]. Therefore, ACQ due to direct π- π interaction is suppressed and solid-state fluorescence of the D-CDs is realized.

To verify our inference, o-phenylenediamine was introduced in the reaction system as an alternative precursor to KHP. Similarly, rigid benzene ring (o-phenylenediamine) and reducing solvent (formaldehyde) were provided without introduction of sp3C. Unsurprisingly, the resulting CDs powder also exerts solid-state fluorescence. The characteristic peak of sp3C also appears in the 13C NMR spectrum of the CDs synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as the precursor (Fig. S3). Accordingly, the solid-state fluorescence of the D-CDs powder is considered to be derived from the doping of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure obtained by the reduction effect of formaldehyde on the precursor containing benzene rings.

The D-CDs are stable in the solid state. The fluorescence intensity of the D-CDs powder decreases only 3.6% after storage for 3 months at room temperature (Fig. S4). The D-CDs possess an excellent photo-stability. As seen from Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of the D-CDs is not remarkably changed after 60 min of irradiation (365 nm). As shown in Fig. S6, although the fluorescence intensity of the D-CDs decreases as pH value lower than 3 or higher than 12, it shows excellent stability in pH from 3 to 12, which ensures their practical applications. Such fluorescence stability of CDs within a certain range of pH has already been reported in the literature [61,62]. In the case of the D-CDs, with pH varying, the protonation or deprotonation of abundant functional groups of the D-CDs occurs and a “protective shell” with positive or negative charge is formed on the surface of the D-CDs, which protect the D-CDs from the attack of acid and base in a certain range of pH (3–12) [63,64]. However, when pH exceeds the protective range (<3 or >12) of the protective shell, the D-CDs may be attacked, accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. TG measurement (Fig. S7) in the range of 30–800 °C was carried out to confirm the thermal stability of the D-CDs. D-CDs only have a slight mass loss below 120 °C (maximum LED junction temperature) [65] and start to loss mass obviously from 500 °C. Nearly 62% of the total weight of the D-CDs is remained during the entire range of applied temperature, which satisfies the application conditions for LED.

Although a few CDs have been applied to fabrication of LED, most of them are limited by the reduction of QY and color variation in the solid state. Fig. 1B presents a comparison of fluorescence spectra for the D-CDs solution and D-CDs powder. Fluorescence spectrum of the D-CDs powder is identical to that of the D-CDs solution, which provides an excellent advantage for the construction of LED. Besides, the D-CDs powder exhibits strong blue solid-state fluorescence with a QY of 67.7%. Therefore, the D-CDs powder can be directly applied as phosphor to fabricate LED.

In order to investigate the photoelectric stability of the LED, the LED sample 3 was operating at different voltages. As shown in Fig. 4C, optimum luminance of the LED sample 3 reaches 3922 cd m−2 at operating voltage of 3.6 V and the emission wavelength of the LED is completely constant irrespective of the operating voltage. The corresponding photoelectric characteristics of the LED sample 3 operating at different voltage including current efficiency (CE), power efficiency (PE), external quantum efficiency (ηext), CIE coordinate, correlated color temperatures (CCT) and CRI value are summarized in Table 4 . As the operating voltage increased from 3.2 V to 4.8 V, the photoelectric characteristics of LED sample 3 only shows a slight difference, indicating the reasonable stability of the LED against the operating voltage.

In summary, a simple and low cost strategy was proposed for preparing the D-CDs powder. The D-CDs powder exhibits a large production yield of 67.8% and a QY of 67.7% without any complicated post-processing steps. The D-CDs powder possessed comparable QY and the same fluorescence wavelength compared with the D-CDs solution. The incorporation of diamond-like carbon (sp3C) structure effectively inhibits the ACQ effect in the D-CDs powder. The D-CDs powder is ideal for direct fabrication of high-efficiency LED as phosphors. The study successfully paved a new avenue for the development of CDs with solid-state fluorescence and the construction of solid-state lighting devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology (No. QAK201809), Development Program of the Ministry of Science and Technology of Jilin Province, China (No. 20180201011GX), and the National Natural Science Fund of China (No. 51778175).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Revised Supporting Information Revised Supporting Information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.04.074.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards high-powered remote wled based on flexible white-luminescent polymer composite films containing S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots
